headline: Scientists Have a Real Word for What a Black Hole Does to You: Spaghettification
body: If you ever drifted too close to a black hole, you'd experience something scientists actually named "spaghettification." The intense gravity squeezes you horizontally and stretches you vertically until you resemble a noodle. It's not just a fun word — it's a real term used by NASA scientists to describe what happens to any matter that crosses the point of no return near a black hole.
summary: Black holes stretch matter into noodle shapes — and scientists have a real word for it.

headline: Every Black Hole Spins — And One Does It Over 1000 Times a Second
body: All black holes spin. That's already a strange thing to sit with — but one called GRS 1915+105 does it at over 1,000 rotations every second. Not per minute. Per second. It's one of the most extreme objects in the known universe, and it just keeps spinning, somewhere out there, right now.
summary: All black holes spin and the fastest known one rotates over 1000 times per second.

headline: If the Sun Became a Black Hole Tomorrow Earth Would Stay in Orbit
body: Here's something that might surprise you: if the Sun were somehow replaced by a black hole of the exact same mass, the planets wouldn't get sucked in. They'd keep orbiting just as they do today — only without any light or heat. Black holes don't vacuum up everything around them. From a safe distance, their gravity works just like any other object of the same mass.
summary: A black hole with the Sun's mass wouldn't pull Earth in — the planets would just keep orbiting in the dark.

headline: Some Black Holes Spin More Than 1,000 Times Every Second
body: All black holes spin, but the fastest known one, GRS 1915+105, rotates more than 1,000 times per second. Even among black holes, that makes it an extreme cosmic object.
summary: The fastest known black hole spins over 1,000 times every second.

headline: Venus Is Hot Enough to Melt Lead
body: Even though Venus isn't the closest planet to the Sun, it's actually the hottest planet in the entire solar system. Its surface gets so incredibly hot that temperatures are high enough to melt lead, turning the planet into what NASA describes as an inferno.
summary: Venus is the hottest planet in the solar system and can melt lead.

headline: Our Closest Neighbor Is Anything but Friendly
body: Venus is Earth's nearest planetary neighbor, but visiting it would be anything but welcoming. The planet is wrapped in thick clouds and hides a surface so extreme that ordinary metals like lead would melt there.
summary: Venus is Earth's closest planetary neighbor but has an incredibly hostile environment.

headline: Venus Hosted Humanity's First Planetary Visit
body: Humans reached Venus surprisingly early in the Space Age. On December 14, 1962, NASA's Mariner 2 spacecraft became the first spacecraft ever to successfully visit another planet when it flew past Venus.
summary: Mariner 2 made the first successful visit to another planet in 1962.

headline: Exploring Venus Has Never Been Easy
body: Dozens of spacecraft have been launched toward Venus over the years, but not every mission managed to succeed, showing just how challenging it can be to study our neighboring world.
summary: Many missions have attempted to explore Venus but not all have succeeded.

headline: Mariner 10 Captured Venus While Leaving
body: One of the first close-up looks at Venus came from NASA's Mariner 10 spacecraft. As the probe sped away from the planet, it captured images that helped reveal what our cloud-covered neighbor looks like from space.
summary: Mariner 10 photographed Venus while flying away from the planet.

headline: Venus Bears the Marks of Volcanoes and Impacts
body: Scientists have uncovered evidence that Venus has experienced both volcanic activity and asteroid impacts. Images from NASA's Magellan mission show areas where volcanic layers and impact craters overlap, revealing a complicated history written across the planet's surface.
summary: Images show that Venus has been shaped by both volcanoes and asteroid impacts.

headline: Multiple Spacecraft Helped Reveal Venus
body: Much of what we know about Venus's surface comes from combining information gathered by different spacecraft. NASA created detailed views of the planet using data collected by both the Magellan spacecraft and the Pioneer Venus Orbiter.
summary: Scientists combined data from multiple spacecraft to map Venus's surface.

headline: NASA Is Sending Two More Missions to Venus
body: Venus has been studied for decades, and NASA isn't finished with it yet. The agency is currently developing two new missions called VERITAS and DAVINCI, which are designed to uncover even more secrets about the mysterious world.
summary: NASA is developing the VERITAS and DAVINCI missions to study Venus.

headline: Venus Hides an Inferno Beneath Its Clouds
body: Venus may look smooth and peaceful from space because of its thick cloud cover, but beneath those clouds lies a harsh world with extreme temperatures and a landscape shaped by volcanoes and ancient impacts.
summary: Venus's calm appearance hides a scorching and violent world underneath.

headline: Thick Clouds Kept Venus a Mystery for Centuries
body: Despite being one of the easiest planets to spot in the sky, Venus remained mysterious for centuries because its dense blanket of clouds hides the surface below. It took spacecraft missions to finally begin revealing what was underneath.
summary: Spacecraft were needed to uncover the hidden surface of Venus.

headline: Mars Is Entirely Inhabited by Robots
body: Mars is the only planet we know of that is inhabited entirely by robots. While humans have never set foot there, robotic explorers have been studying the Red Planet for decades, acting as pioneers for future astronauts.
summary: Mars is the only known planet populated entirely by robots.

headline: Robots Are Preparing the Way for Humans
body: NASA's robotic explorers, including Perseverance and the Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter, are serving as pathfinders for future astronauts. Their discoveries are helping scientists understand the planet and prepare for eventual human missions.
summary: NASA's robotic explorers are helping pave the way for future human missions to Mars.

headline: Two Rovers Are Exploring Billions of Years of History
body: NASA's Curiosity and Perseverance rovers are exploring regions about 2,300 miles apart, but together they're studying billions of years of Martian history. Their discoveries are helping scientists understand how Mars formed and changed over time.
summary: Curiosity and Perseverance are uncovering billions of years of Martian history.

headline: Mars Used to Be Warmer and Wetter
body: Evidence collected by NASA missions suggests that billions of years ago Mars had a thicker atmosphere and was much warmer and wetter than it is today. Scientists continue studying the planet to understand how dramatically it changed.
summary: Mars may once have been a much warmer and wetter world.

headline: Mars May Once Have Hosted Microbial Life
body: Scientists are investigating whether Mars was once home to ancient microbial life. Discoveries made by rovers and orbiters continue to provide clues about whether the Red Planet may have been habitable billions of years ago.
summary: Scientists are searching for signs that ancient microbial life may once have existed on Mars.

headline: A Rover Has Used AI to Drive on Another Planet
body: NASA's Perseverance rover completed the first artificial intelligence-planned drives on another planet. Instead of relying entirely on human operators, generative AI helped create routes that allowed the rover to navigate more autonomously.
summary: Perseverance became the first rover to use AI-planned driving on another planet.

headline: Perseverance Is Collecting Samples for a Round Trip
body: The Perseverance rover is the first step in a proposed roundtrip mission that aims to bring samples from Mars back to Earth. Scientists hope these samples could answer major questions about the planet's past.
summary: Perseverance is collecting samples that may eventually be returned to Earth.

headline: Mars Is One of the Most Explored Places in Space
body: Mars is one of the most explored bodies in the solar system and the only planet where humans have sent rovers to explore the surface. Multiple orbiters and rovers are actively studying the Red Planet today.
summary: Mars is one of the most explored worlds in our solar system.

headline: Mars Is Humanity's Horizon Goal
body: NASA considers Mars a major destination for human exploration because it is one of the few places in the solar system where life may have once existed. Studying Mars could teach us about Earth's past and whether life exists elsewhere.
summary: Mars remains one of humanity's most important destinations for exploration.

headline: A One-Year Mission Lasted More Than Eleven Years
body: NASA's MAVEN mission was originally designed for just one year of science operations around Mars. Instead, the spacecraft spent more than eleven years studying the Martian atmosphere before the mission finally came to an end.
summary: NASA's MAVEN spacecraft far exceeded its original mission duration.

headline: Black Holes Aren't Actually Holes
body: Despite their name, black holes aren't really holes at all. They're enormous amounts of matter packed into incredibly tiny spaces. Their gravity is so powerful that nothing, not even light, can escape once it crosses a boundary called the event horizon.
summary: Black holes are extremely dense objects rather than actual holes.

headline: Not Even Light Can Escape a Black Hole
body: A black hole is so dense that gravity just beneath its event horizon becomes strong enough that nothing can escape its pull. Even light, the fastest thing in the universe, cannot get back out.
summary: The gravity near a black hole is so strong that even light cannot escape.

headline: The Event Horizon Isn't a Surface
body: The event horizon is often imagined as the surface of a black hole, but it isn't a physical surface like Earth's ground or the Sun's outer layers. Instead, it's a boundary that contains all the matter making up the black hole.
summary: The event horizon is a boundary rather than a physical surface.

headline: Black Holes Are Invisible to Telescopes
body: Black holes don't emit or reflect light, making them effectively invisible. Scientists usually find them by studying how they affect nearby stars, gas, dust, and even space-time itself.
summary: Scientists detect black holes by observing their effects on the universe around them.

headline: Spaghettification Is a Real Scientific Term
body: Scientists actually use the term 'spaghettification' to describe what happens when matter gets too close to a black hole. The immense gravity squeezes objects horizontally while stretching them vertically, turning them into something resembling noodles.
summary: Spaghettification is the stretching and squeezing of matter near a black hole.

headline: Black Holes Aren't Cosmic Vacuum Cleaners
body: Despite what movies often show, black holes don't go around sucking up everything nearby. From far enough away, their gravity behaves just like any other object with the same mass.
summary: Black holes don't indiscriminately pull in everything around them.

headline: Earth Would Keep Orbiting a Black Hole Sun
body: If the Sun were somehow replaced with a black hole of the exact same mass, the planets would continue orbiting almost exactly as they do now. The solar system would become much colder, but Earth's orbit wouldn't suddenly collapse.
summary: Replacing the Sun with an equally massive black hole wouldn't destroy the planets' orbits.

headline: Black Holes Can Bend Light Like a Cosmic Magnifying Glass
body: Black holes are so massive that they can bend and distort light coming from objects behind them. This phenomenon, known as gravitational lensing, helps scientists detect black holes that would otherwise remain invisible.
summary: Black holes can warp light and reveal themselves through gravitational lensing.

headline: Our Galaxy Contains a Monster Black Hole
body: Most galaxies the size of the Milky Way contain gigantic black holes at their centers. The one at the center of our own galaxy, Sagittarius A*, has a mass roughly four million times greater than the Sun.
summary: The Milky Way contains a supermassive black hole called Sagittarius A*.

headline: Asteroids Are Older Than Earth
body: Asteroids are rocky remnants left over from the formation of our solar system about 4.6 billion years ago. They are essentially cosmic leftovers that have survived since before Earth fully formed.
summary: Asteroids are ancient leftovers from the birth of the solar system.

headline: All the Asteroids Together Weigh Less Than the Moon
body: There are millions of asteroids in our solar system, but if you combined all of them into one object, their total mass would still be less than that of Earth's Moon.
summary: The combined mass of all asteroids is smaller than Earth's Moon.

headline: Asteroids Range From Mountains to Small Houses
body: Asteroids come in an enormous range of sizes. The largest, Vesta, is about 329 miles wide, while some asteroids measure less than 33 feet across, making them smaller than many houses.
summary: Asteroids range in size from giant worlds to objects smaller than houses.

headline: NASA Has a Planetary Defense Office
body: NASA operates a Planetary Defense Coordination Office whose job is to find, track, and better understand asteroids and comets that could pose an impact hazard to Earth.
summary: NASA has an office dedicated to protecting Earth from dangerous asteroids.

headline: NASA Changed an Asteroid's Orbit on Purpose
body: NASA's DART spacecraft intentionally crashed into the asteroid moonlet Dimorphos. Later research showed the impact changed the orbit of the asteroid system, proving spacecraft can alter the motion of small worlds.
summary: NASA's DART mission changed the orbit of an asteroid system.

headline: Most Asteroids Live Between Mars and Jupiter
body: Most asteroids orbit the Sun in a region called the main asteroid belt, located between Mars and Jupiter. It is the solar system's best-known gathering place for rocky leftovers from planetary formation.
summary: Most asteroids are found in the main asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.

headline: NASA Constantly Tracks Potentially Dangerous Asteroids
body: Scientists at NASA's Center for Near Earth Object Studies calculate extremely precise orbits for asteroids and other near-Earth objects to help identify possible future impact threats.
summary: NASA continuously tracks asteroids that could someday threaten Earth.

headline: Robots Have Visited Asteroids Up Close
body: NASA has sent multiple robotic spacecraft to visit asteroids and study their composition and structure. These missions allow scientists to investigate some of the oldest objects in the solar system.
summary: Robotic spacecraft have explored asteroids up close.

headline: There Are More Planets Than Stars in Our Galaxy
body: Scientists now believe there are more planets than stars in the Milky Way. With billions of stars already present, that means our galaxy may contain hundreds of billions of worlds.
summary: The Milky Way likely contains more planets than stars.

headline: We've Confirmed More Than 6,000 Alien Worlds
body: NASA has confirmed more than 6,000 exoplanets orbiting distant stars. Scientists believe this is only a tiny fraction of the billions of planets that exist throughout the galaxy.
summary: Scientists have confirmed more than 6,000 planets outside our solar system.

headline: Some Planets Wander Through Space Alone
body: Not every planet orbits a star. Some exoplanets, known as rogue planets, drift freely through space without being gravitationally bound to any star at all.
summary: Some planets travel through space without orbiting a star.

headline: Scientists Have Found Lava Worlds and Styrofoam Worlds
body: Exoplanets come in astonishing varieties. Researchers have discovered planets covered in molten oceans, worlds made mostly of iron, and even planets with densities comparable to Styrofoam.
summary: Alien planets come in forms stranger than scientists once imagined.

headline: Some Planets Live in the Goldilocks Zone
body: Astronomers search for planets in the habitable zone, often called the Goldilocks zone, where temperatures may be just right for liquid water to exist on the surface.
summary: The Goldilocks zone is where conditions may be suitable for liquid water.

headline: Finding Alien Life Would Change Humanity Forever
body: NASA describes the question of whether life exists beyond Earth as one of the most profound questions of all time. Scientists believe that whatever the answer turns out to be, it would permanently change humanity's understanding of its place in the universe.
summary: Discovering life beyond Earth would transform humanity's view of the universe.

headline: One Star Has Seven Earth-Sized Planets
body: The TRAPPIST-1 system contains seven Earth-sized planets orbiting a small red dwarf star. Located about 40 light-years away, it is one of the most intriguing planetary systems discovered so far.
summary: TRAPPIST-1 hosts seven Earth-sized planets.

headline: The Closest Known Exoplanet Is Still Four Light-Years Away
body: Proxima Centauri b is the closest known exoplanet to Earth, but it is still about four light-years away. Even our nearest known alien world is separated from us by an enormous cosmic distance.
summary: The nearest known exoplanet is about four light-years from Earth.

headline: Billions of Alien Worlds Are Still Waiting to Be Found
body: Although scientists have confirmed more than 6,000 exoplanets, they estimate billions more remain undiscovered. Every new detection helps researchers understand how common worlds like Earth may be.
summary: Scientists believe billions of exoplanets remain undiscovered.

headline: Ordinary People Have Helped Discover New Worlds
body: Citizen scientists working alongside NASA researchers have contributed to thousands of discoveries. Amateur astronomers and volunteers around the world help analyze data and observe distant planets.
summary: Citizen scientists have played an important role in exoplanet discoveries.

headline: Frozen Comets Can Be the Size of a Small Town
body: When far from the Sun, comets are frozen balls of ice, rock, and dust that are often only about the size of a small town. Despite their modest size, they can create enormous displays when they approach the Sun.
summary: Many comets are no larger than a small town when frozen.

headline: The Oort Cloud May Hold Countless Comets
body: NASA says there are likely billions of comets orbiting the Sun in the Kuiper Belt and the even more distant Oort Cloud. Most of these icy objects remain far beyond the familiar planets.
summary: The distant Oort Cloud may contain huge numbers of comets.

headline: A Comet's Glowing Head Can Become Bigger Than a Planet
body: As a comet approaches the Sun, heat causes gases and dust to erupt from its surface. This creates a glowing head, called a coma, that can grow larger than most planets.
summary: A comet's glowing coma can become larger than planets.

headline: Comet Tails Can Stretch for Millions of Miles
body: The dust and gases released from a comet form tails that point away from the Sun. These spectacular tails can extend for millions of miles across space.
summary: Comet tails can stretch for millions of miles.

headline: There May Be Billions of Comets Around the Sun
body: Scientists believe billions of comets orbit our Sun in regions such as the Kuiper Belt and the distant Oort Cloud. Most of them have never been seen from Earth.
summary: Billions of comets may exist in the outer reaches of the solar system.

headline: A Comet Was Seen Reversing Its Spin for the First Time
body: Using the Hubble Space Telescope, astronomers discovered the first known comet whose rotation slowed down and then reversed direction. It was the first time such behavior had ever been observed.
summary: Astronomers have observed a comet reverse its spin.

headline: Only Three Interstellar Objects Have Ever Been Confirmed
body: Scientists have confirmed only three known objects from outside our solar system entering our cosmic neighborhood. They include 'Oumuamua, 2I/Borisov, and the recently discovered 3I/ATLAS.
summary: Only three confirmed interstellar visitors have been detected so far.

headline: 'Oumuamua Was Humanity's First Known Interstellar Visitor
body: Discovered in 2017, 'Oumuamua became the first known object from another star system ever observed passing through our solar system. Its unusual shape and origin sparked worldwide interest and debate.
summary: 'Oumuamua was the first known visitor from another star system.

headline: Only Five Dwarf Planets Are Officially Recognized
body: The International Astronomical Union currently recognizes only five dwarf planets in our solar system: Ceres, Pluto, Haumea, Makemake, and Eris.
summary: Only five dwarf planets are officially recognized.

headline: Scientists Think Hundreds More Dwarf Planets Exist
body: Although only five have been officially recognized, astronomers believe there could be more than a hundred dwarf planets waiting to be discovered.
summary: There may be hundreds of undiscovered dwarf planets.

headline: Pluto Is Just One Member of a Larger Family
body: Pluto was once considered the ninth planet, but it is now known to be one of several dwarf planets inhabiting the outer solar system.
summary: Pluto is part of a larger family of dwarf planets.

headline: Makemake Is the Second-Brightest Object in the Kuiper Belt
body: Makemake is one of the brightest objects beyond Neptune and is second only to Pluto in brightness among known Kuiper Belt dwarf planets.
summary: Makemake is one of the brightest worlds in the outer solar system.

headline: Ceres Is the Only Dwarf Planet in the Inner Solar System
body: Unlike the other known dwarf planets, Ceres resides between Mars and Jupiter inside the asteroid belt.
summary: Ceres is the only dwarf planet located in the inner solar system.

headline: Pluto Lost Planet Status Because It Couldn't Clear Its Orbit
body: According to the International Astronomical Union, planets must clear their orbital neighborhoods. Pluto failed that requirement and was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.
summary: Pluto became a dwarf planet because it shares its orbit with other objects.

headline: Haumea Is One of the Fastest-Rotating Large Objects in the Solar System
body: Haumea stands out because it is one of the fastest rotating large objects in our solar system. Among dwarf planets, that makes it one of the strangest worlds on NASA's list.
summary: Haumea is one of the fastest rotating large objects in the solar system.

headline: Eris Is One of the Largest Known Dwarf Planets
body: Eris is one of the largest known dwarf planets in our solar system. Its size makes it one of the major worlds in the same category as Pluto, Ceres, Haumea, and Makemake.
summary: Eris is one of the largest known dwarf planets.

headline: Dwarf Planets Are Surprisingly Similar to Regular Planets
body: Dwarf planets orbit the Sun and are nearly round, making them very similar to planets except for one key requirement involving their orbital neighborhoods.
summary: Dwarf planets are much more planet-like than many people realize.

headline: The Official Planet Definition Didn't Exist Until 2006
body: Astronomers only adopted the modern definition of a planet in 2006, creating the category of dwarf planets in the process.
summary: The modern definition of a planet is surprisingly recent.

headline: One Thousand Earths Could Fit Inside Jupiter
body: If Jupiter were hollow, roughly 1,000 Earths could fit inside it, making it by far the largest planet in our solar system.
summary: Jupiter is so large that about 1,000 Earths could fit inside it.

headline: Jupiter Is More Massive Than All the Other Planets Combined
body: Jupiter contains more than twice the mass of all the other planets in the solar system put together.
summary: Jupiter outweighs all the other planets combined.

headline: Jupiter Has the Shortest Day in the Solar System
body: Despite its enormous size, Jupiter rotates once every 9.9 hours, giving it the shortest day of any planet.
summary: Jupiter experiences the shortest day in the solar system.

headline: Jupiter Is the Oldest Planet in the Solar System
body: Scientists believe Jupiter formed first, making it the oldest planet in our solar system.
summary: Jupiter may have been the first planet to form.

headline: Jupiter Is Surrounded by Thousands of Smaller Objects
body: Beyond its moons, Jupiter's gravitational pull controls countless asteroids and smaller objects that orbit alongside the giant planet.
summary: Thousands of objects accompany Jupiter through space.

headline: Galileo Studied Jupiter with a Homemade Telescope
body: In 1610, Galileo Galilei made the first detailed observations of Jupiter using a small telescope he built himself.
summary: Galileo used a homemade telescope to study Jupiter.

headline: Jupiter's Great Red Spot Is a Giant Storm
body: The Great Red Spot is a massive storm on Jupiter that has been raging for centuries and is larger than Earth itself.
summary: Jupiter's famous storm is larger than our entire planet.

headline: Four Giant Moons Orbit Jupiter
body: Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto are Jupiter's four largest moons and were first observed by Galileo over 400 years ago.
summary: Jupiter's four largest moons are worlds of their own.

headline: Jupiter Has 95 Officially Recognized Moons
body: Jupiter has 95 moons officially recognized by the International Astronomical Union. That number only hints at how complex Jupiter's system of moons, rings, and small orbiting objects really is.
summary: Jupiter has 95 officially recognized moons.

headline: Jupiter's Moons Leave Glowing Marks in Its Atmosphere
body: Jupiter's powerful auroras contain signatures left behind by its moons, creating glowing patterns unlike anything seen on Earth.
summary: Jupiter's moons create visible effects inside the planet's auroras.

headline: Saturn Was the Farthest Planet Found by the Naked Eye
body: Saturn is the farthest planet from Earth that was discovered without a telescope. Long before spacecraft visited it, humans could already spot the ringed planet shining in the night sky.
summary: Saturn was the farthest planet discovered by unaided human eyesight.

headline: Only Four Spacecraft Have Visited Saturn
body: Despite being one of the most famous planets, only four robotic spacecraft have visited Saturn: Pioneer 11, Voyager 1, Voyager 2, and Cassini.
summary: Only four robotic spacecraft have visited Saturn.

headline: Cassini Transformed Our Understanding of Saturn
body: NASA's Cassini spacecraft arrived at Saturn in 2004, and that is when scientists say our understanding of the ringed planet really started to take shape.
summary: Cassini dramatically expanded what scientists know about Saturn.

headline: Saturn Has More Official Moons Than Any Other Planet
body: Saturn has dozens of officially recognized moons in its orbit, more than any other planet. Its moon system ranges from giant worlds to tiny objects.
summary: Saturn has more officially recognized moons than any other planet.

headline: Saturn Has a Moon Bigger Than Mercury
body: Titan, Saturn's largest moon, is bigger than the planet Mercury. It is one of the most fascinating moons in the solar system.
summary: Titan is larger than Mercury.

headline: One Saturn Moon Is as Small as a Sports Arena
body: Saturn's moons vary wildly in size. Some are enormous, like Titan, while one is described by NASA as being as small as a sports arena.
summary: Saturn has moons ranging from giant worlds to sports-arena-sized objects.

headline: Enceladus Has an Ocean Hidden Under Ice
body: Saturn's moon Enceladus has a global ocean beneath a thick, icy shell. That makes it one of the most intriguing ocean worlds in the solar system.
summary: Enceladus hides a global ocean under its icy surface.

headline: Enceladus Shoots Giant Plumes of Water Into Space
body: Scientists discovered that Saturn's moon Enceladus sprays enormous jets of water vapor and ice particles into space through cracks in its frozen surface.
summary: Enceladus ejects giant plumes of water into space.

headline: Uranus Spins Sideways
body: Uranus rotates at nearly a 90-degree angle from the plane of its orbit. This extreme tilt makes the planet appear to spin on its side.
summary: Uranus appears to spin sideways because of its extreme tilt.

headline: Uranus Has Rings Too
body: Saturn may be famous for its rings, but Uranus also has a faint ring system. In telescope images, those rings can appear wrapped around the sideways-spinning planet.
summary: Uranus has faint rings of its own.

headline: Only One Spacecraft Has Ever Visited Uranus
body: Voyager 2 is the only spacecraft that has ever explored Uranus up close. It flew past the planet in January 1986 and returned images and data scientists still study today.
summary: Only Voyager 2 has visited Uranus up close.

headline: Voyager 2 Found New Moons and Rings at Uranus
body: During its Uranus encounter, Voyager 2 discovered 10 new moons, two new rings, and a magnetic field stronger than Saturn's.
summary: Voyager 2 discovered moons, rings, and a strong magnetic field at Uranus.

headline: Uranus Has Literary Moons
body: Uranus's moons are sometimes called literary moons because many are named after characters from Shakespeare, along with some from the works of Alexander Pope.
summary: Many Uranus moons are named after literary characters.

headline: Old Voyager Data Still Reveals Uranus Mysteries
body: Decades after Voyager 2 became the first and only spacecraft to visit Uranus, scientists are still mining its old data to solve mysteries about the ice giant.
summary: Voyager 2 data continues to reveal new insights about Uranus.

headline: Neptune Was Discovered With Math Before Anyone Saw It
body: More than 200 years after Galileo accidentally recorded Neptune as a star, astronomers used mathematical predictions to locate the planet before anyone had directly identified it in the sky.
summary: Neptune became the first planet discovered through mathematics.

headline: The Sun Would Look Tiny From Neptune
body: Because Neptune orbits more than 30 times farther from the Sun than Earth, the Sun appears much smaller and dimmer in Neptune's skies.
summary: The Sun would appear tiny and faint from Neptune.

headline: Only One Spacecraft Has Ever Visited Neptune
body: NASA's Voyager 2 became the first spacecraft to study Neptune up close in 1989 and remains the only spacecraft ever to visit the planet.
summary: Voyager 2 is still humanity's only visitor to Neptune.

headline: Galileo Accidentally Saw Neptune More Than 200 Years Early
body: Galileo recorded Neptune through his small telescope in 1612 and 1613, but mistook it for a fixed star. The planet would not officially be discovered until more than two centuries later.
summary: Galileo unknowingly observed Neptune centuries before its discovery.

headline: Neptune's Largest Moon Was Found Just 17 Days Later
body: Astronomers discovered Triton, Neptune's largest moon, only 17 days after Neptune itself was discovered in 1846.
summary: Triton was discovered just days after Neptune.

headline: Neptune Is Whipped by Supersonic Winds
body: Neptune experiences powerful winds that can reach supersonic speeds, making them some of the fastest winds found anywhere in the solar system.
summary: Neptune is home to incredibly fast winds.

headline: Neptune Marks the Edge of the Planetary Neighborhood
body: Neptune is the eighth and most distant major planet from the Sun, forming the outer frontier of the planets in our solar system.
summary: Neptune sits at the edge of the major planets.

headline: Humans Have Visited Neptune Exactly Once
body: Humanity has explored Neptune only once, when Voyager 2 flew past the planet in 1989. No spacecraft has returned since.
summary: Neptune has been visited only once.

headline: You Can't See Neptune Without a Telescope
body: Despite being a giant planet, Neptune is so distant from Earth that it cannot be seen with the naked eye.
summary: Neptune is too far away to be seen without a telescope.

headline: Pluto Was Once the Ninth Planet
body: For decades, Pluto was taught as the ninth planet in the solar system before being reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.
summary: Pluto was once considered the solar system's ninth planet.

headline: A Spacecraft Has Visited Pluto Only Once
body: NASA's New Horizons mission remains the only spacecraft to have explored Pluto up close.
summary: Pluto has only been visited once by a spacecraft.

headline: New Horizons Was the First Spacecraft to Explore Pluto
body: In 2015, NASA's New Horizons became the first spacecraft ever to fly past Pluto and reveal the dwarf planet in stunning detail.
summary: New Horizons gave humanity its first close look at Pluto.

headline: Pluto Lives in the Kuiper Belt
body: Pluto resides in the Kuiper Belt, a distant region beyond Neptune filled with icy objects left over from the formation of the solar system.
summary: Pluto is part of the Kuiper Belt beyond Neptune.

headline: Pluto's Largest Moon Is Almost Half Its Size
body: Pluto's moon Charon is about half the size of Pluto itself, making it the largest known moon relative to its parent world in the solar system.
summary: Charon is unusually large compared to Pluto.

headline: Pluto and Charon Form One of the Strangest Pairs in Space
body: Because Charon is so large compared to Pluto, the two worlds form one of the most unusual planet-moon systems in the solar system.
summary: Pluto and Charon make an unusually balanced pair.

headline: New Horizons Explored the Solar System's Third Zone
body: After flying past Pluto, New Horizons became the first mission to explore the Kuiper Belt, sometimes called the solar system's third zone beyond the giant planets.
summary: New Horizons opened humanity's exploration of the Kuiper Belt.

headline: Pluto Was Officially Demoted in 2006
body: The International Astronomical Union reclassified Pluto as a dwarf planet in 2006, ending its status as the solar system's ninth planet.
summary: Pluto officially became a dwarf planet in 2006.

headline: Pluto Has a Giant Heart Visible From Space
body: Images from New Horizons revealed a massive heart-shaped region on Pluto's surface that quickly became one of the most recognizable features in the solar system.
summary: Pluto's famous heart-shaped feature is visible from space.

headline: Our Solar System Is Traveling Around the Milky Way
body: Our solar system is constantly moving through the Milky Way galaxy as it circles the galactic center.
summary: The solar system is orbiting the center of the Milky Way.

headline: One Trip Around the Galaxy Takes 230 Million Years
body: It takes our solar system about 230 million years to complete a single orbit around the center of the Milky Way galaxy.
summary: A single trip around the galaxy takes 230 million years.

headline: More Than 300 Spacecraft Have Left Earth
body: More than 300 robotic spacecraft have traveled beyond Earth's orbit to explore the solar system.
summary: Hundreds of spacecraft have ventured beyond Earth.

headline: Only 24 Humans Have Ever Traveled to the Moon
body: Despite decades of exploration, only 24 people have ever journeyed from Earth to the Moon.
summary: Just 24 humans have traveled to the Moon.

headline: Earth Is Still the Only World Known to Host Life
body: Among all the worlds in our solar system, Earth remains the only place where life has been discovered.
summary: Earth is the only known home of life in the solar system.

headline: Planets Aren't the Only Objects With Rings
body: The four giant planets aren't the only ringed worlds. At least one asteroid also possesses its own ring system.
summary: Rings aren't exclusive to giant planets.

headline: We Live in a Small Branch of the Milky Way
body: Our solar system resides inside a spiral arm of the Milky Way known as the Orion Spur.
summary: The solar system sits inside the Orion Spur.

headline: No Two Planetary Skies Are Alike
body: From Venus' crushing atmosphere to the Moon's near vacuum, worlds across the solar system have dramatically different skies and environments.
summary: Worlds throughout the solar system have wildly different atmospheres.

headline: The Sun Holds the Solar System Together
body: The Sun's gravity keeps everything in the solar system in orbit, from giant planets to tiny bits of debris.
summary: The Sun's gravity binds the solar system together.

headline: Earth's Weather Depends on the Sun
body: The Sun influences seasons, weather patterns, climate, ocean currents, and many other processes on Earth.
summary: Earth's environment is deeply connected to the Sun.

headline: Billions of Stars Are Similar to Our Sun
body: Although the Sun is special to us, astronomers believe billions of similar stars are scattered throughout the Milky Way.
summary: The Milky Way contains billions of stars similar to the Sun.

headline: The Word 'Solar' Comes From Latin
body: The adjective 'solar' comes from the Latin word 'sol,' an ancient name for the Sun.
summary: The word solar has Latin origins.

headline: Ancient Civilizations Built Myths Around the Sun
body: Cultures around the world have woven the Sun into myths, religions, and traditions for thousands of years.
summary: The Sun has inspired cultures for millennia.

headline: Superman Gets His Powers From Our Sun
body: According to comic book lore, Superman and other Kryptonians gain enhanced abilities from the yellow light of our Sun.
summary: Comic book heroes draw inspiration from our Sun.

headline: Ordinary People Can Help Discover Comets
body: Citizen scientists can participate in NASA's Sungrazer Project to help identify comets that pass close to the Sun.
summary: Anyone can help search for comets near the Sun.

headline: Some Comets Die in the Sun
body: Certain comets are destroyed after venturing too close to the Sun, disappearing in spectacular fashion.
summary: Some comets are consumed by the Sun.

headline: The Sun Helps Create Earth's Auroras
body: Interactions between the Sun and Earth influence radiation belts and produce the auroras seen near the poles.
summary: Auroras are connected to activity from the Sun.

headline: Some Galaxies Contain Trillions of Stars
body: The largest galaxies in the universe can contain trillions of stars held together by gravity.
summary: Some galaxies contain trillions of stars.

headline: Most Large Galaxies Hide Giant Black Holes
body: Most large galaxies harbor supermassive black holes at their centers, some billions of times more massive than the Sun.
summary: Most galaxies hide enormous black holes.

headline: Some Galaxies Are Almost as Old as the Universe
body: Astronomers have discovered galaxies that formed shortly after the universe itself came into existence.
summary: Some galaxies are nearly as old as the universe.

headline: The Milky Way Is More Than 100,000 Light-Years Wide
body: Our home galaxy stretches over 100,000 light-years across.
summary: The Milky Way spans over 100,000 light-years.

headline: The Milky Way Lives in a Galactic Neighborhood
body: Our galaxy is part of a community called the Local Group, surrounded by dozens of neighboring galaxies.
summary: The Milky Way has many galactic neighbors.

headline: The Milky Way Paints a Stripe Across the Sky
body: Seen from Earth, our galaxy appears as a faint glowing band stretching across the night sky.
summary: The Milky Way appears as a band in the sky.

headline: Galaxies Form a Giant Cosmic Web
body: Galaxies cluster together into enormous structures called the cosmic web, spanning vast regions of the universe.
summary: Galaxies are connected through the cosmic web.

headline: Some Galaxies Have Only a Few Thousand Stars
body: Tiny dwarf galaxies can contain only a few thousand stars compared to the trillions found in larger galaxies.
summary: Some galaxies are surprisingly small.

headline: There May Be One Septillion Stars in the Universe
body: Astronomers estimate that the universe could contain up to one septillion stars, a number with 24 zeros.
summary: The universe may contain one septillion stars.

headline: Some Stars Can Live for Trillions of Years
body: Certain low-mass stars burn their fuel so slowly that they could survive for trillions of years.
summary: Some stars can outlive the current age of the universe.

headline: Some Stars Burn Out in Just Millions of Years
body: Massive stars consume their fuel rapidly and may survive only a few million years.
summary: Some stars live surprisingly short lives.

headline: Stars Are Born Inside Giant Stellar Nurseries
body: New stars form inside vast clouds of gas and dust known as stellar nurseries.
summary: Stars begin life inside giant nurseries.

headline: Our Sun Is About Halfway Through Its Life
body: The Sun is roughly midway through its long main-sequence stage.
summary: The Sun is middle-aged.

headline: Stars Help Build Future Worlds
body: Elements produced inside stars eventually become part of future stars, planets, and living things.
summary: Stars create ingredients for future worlds.

headline: Some Stars Die in Giant Explosions
body: Massive stars can end their lives in spectacular explosions called supernovae.
summary: Some stars explode when they die.

headline: Dead Stars Can Become Black Holes
body: The collapsed cores of some massive stars can eventually form black holes.
summary: Dead stars can become black holes.

headline: Some Dead Stars Are the Size of Earth
body: White dwarfs are stellar remnants roughly the size of Earth, despite containing immense amounts of matter.
summary: Some stars end up Earth-sized.

headline: The Stars in a Constellation Aren't Actually Connected
body: Stars that appear together in a constellation may be separated by enormous distances and are not physically connected at all.
summary: Constellations are optical patterns rather than physical groups.

headline: Different Cultures Invented Different Constellations
body: People around the world imagined different shapes and stories in the stars, creating their own constellations over thousands of years.
summary: Cultures around the world created different constellations.

headline: The Night Sky Changes Throughout the Year
body: Because Earth orbits the Sun, different constellations become visible as the seasons change.
summary: The stars you see depend on the time of year.

headline: The Big Dipper Isn't What Most People Think
body: The Big Dipper is one of the most famous patterns in the sky, but it's actually just part of the larger constellation Ursa Major.
summary: The Big Dipper is only part of a constellation.

headline: Meteor Showers Are Named After Constellations
body: Meteor showers get their names from the constellations where the meteors appear to originate.
summary: Meteor showers are linked to constellations.

headline: Sailors Used Stars as GPS for Centuries
body: Long before modern technology, sailors navigated the oceans by using the stars.
summary: People once relied on stars for navigation.

headline: NASA Astronauts Still Train to Navigate by the Stars
body: Astronauts practice celestial navigation as a backup in case modern navigation systems fail.
summary: Astronauts still learn ancient navigation techniques.

headline: Spacecraft Use Star Maps to Find Their Way
body: Modern spacecraft carry star maps and compare them with images of the sky to determine their orientation.
summary: Spacecraft navigate using the stars.

headline: People in Different Hemispheres See Different Constellations
body: Observers in the Northern and Southern Hemispheres see slightly different skies because Earth points them in different directions.
summary: Your location on Earth affects which constellations you can see.

headline: Some Nebulae Are Giant Star Nurseries
body: Many nebulae are enormous regions where new stars are actively forming.
summary: Some nebulae give birth to stars.

headline: Some Nebulae Are the Remains of Dead Stars
body: Certain nebulae formed from gas and dust expelled by dying stars after supernova explosions.
summary: Some nebulae are created by dying stars.

headline: Gravity Turns Clouds Into Stars
body: Gravity slowly pulls together gas and dust inside nebulae until baby stars begin to form.
summary: Gravity transforms clouds into stars.

headline: Even the Closest Nebula Is Incredibly Far Away
body: The nearest known nebula, the Helix Nebula, is so distant that light from it takes about 700 years to reach Earth.
summary: Even nearby nebulae are unimaginably far away.

headline: Some Nebulae Look Like Giant Cosmic Mountains
body: Images from powerful telescopes reveal towering clouds inside nebulae that resemble mountains and valleys.
summary: Nebulae can resemble landscapes.

headline: One Nebula Looks Like a Giant Eyeball
body: The Helix Nebula's appearance has earned it comparisons to a massive eye staring back from space.
summary: The Helix Nebula resembles a giant eye.

headline: Webb Can See Baby Stars Hidden Inside Nebulae
body: Infrared images from the James Webb Space Telescope reveal regions of star birth that are invisible in ordinary light.
summary: Webb can see hidden star-forming regions.

headline: Baby Stars Begin in the Hearts of Nebulae
body: As gas clouds collapse under gravity, the material at their centers heats up and becomes a newborn star.
summary: Stars begin deep inside nebulae.

headline: Nebulae Float Between the Stars
body: Nebulae occupy interstellar space, the vast regions that exist between stars.
summary: Nebulae are found in the spaces between stars.

headline: Some Asteroids Have Their Own Moons
body: Not only planets, but even some asteroids possess their own natural satellites.
summary: Even asteroids can have moons.

headline: Pluto and Charon Almost Form a Double Planet
body: Pluto's largest moon, Charon, is so massive relative to Pluto that the pair nearly behaves like a double-planet system.
summary: Pluto and Charon are unusually similar in size.

headline: Earth's Moon Helps Keep Our Planet Stable
body: The Moon moderates Earth's wobble, helping maintain a more stable climate over long periods.
summary: The Moon helps make Earth more livable.

headline: Some Moons Have Their Own Atmospheres
body: While many moons are airless, a few possess atmospheres of varying thickness and composition.
summary: Not all moons are airless rocks.

headline: Some of Saturn's Moons Look Like Ravioli
body: Tiny moons orbiting Saturn have strange flattened shapes that resemble pieces of ravioli.
summary: Some moons have bizarre shapes.

headline: Uranus Names Its Moons After Literature
body: Many of Uranus' moons are named after characters from Shakespeare and Alexander Pope.
summary: Uranus takes moon names from literature.

headline: Some Moons Might Be Suitable for Life
body: Scientists believe a few moons in the solar system may possess conditions that could support life.
summary: Some moons may be habitable.

headline: One Moon Is Bigger Than Mercury
body: Jupiter's moon Ganymede is larger than the planet Mercury, making it the biggest moon in the solar system.
summary: Ganymede is bigger than Mercury.

headline: Mars Has Two Potato-Shaped Moons
body: Mars' tiny moons, Phobos and Deimos, have irregular shapes that resemble potatoes.
summary: Mars' moons look surprisingly lumpy.

headline: Supernovas Are the Biggest Explosions Humans Have Ever Seen
body: Supernovas are the most powerful explosions ever observed by humanity.
summary: Supernovas are the universe's greatest fireworks.

headline: Some Exploding Stars Outshine Entire Galaxies
body: A single supernova can shine brighter than all the stars in its galaxy combined for days or months.
summary: One star can temporarily outshine billions of others.

headline: A Giant Star Can Collapse in Just Seconds
body: The core of a massive star can collapse in roughly 15 seconds before triggering a supernova explosion.
summary: Massive stars can die incredibly quickly.

headline: Supernovas Helped Create the Gold on Earth
body: Heavy elements like gold were forged inside stars and distributed across space by supernovas.
summary: Your jewelry was born in exploding stars.

headline: Dead Stars Can Explode After Stealing From Neighbors
body: White dwarfs can trigger supernovas after accumulating too much material from nearby companion stars.
summary: Dead stars sometimes explode after stealing matter.

headline: Supernovas Can Create Black Holes
body: Some of the most massive stars leave behind black holes after exploding.
summary: Exploding stars can produce black holes.

headline: Humans Witnessed the Birth of the Crab Nebula
body: Observers recorded the supernova that created the Crab Nebula nearly a thousand years ago.
summary: People saw the Crab Nebula being born.

headline: Supernovas Are Surprisingly Rare
body: Despite their immense power, supernovas occur surprisingly infrequently inside galaxies like the Milky Way.
summary: These enormous explosions are rarer than many people realize.

headline: Some Dead Stars Are the Size of Earth
body: White dwarfs pack stellar mass into objects roughly the size of Earth.
summary: Dead stars can shrink to Earth-sized objects.

headline: Scientists Use Exploding Stars Like Rulers
body: Astronomers use certain supernovas to measure vast distances across the universe.
summary: Supernovas help scientists measure the cosmos.

headline: We Live Inside a City of Stars
body: Our solar system is just one tiny neighborhood inside the Milky Way, a galaxy containing roughly 100 billion stars.
summary: The Milky Way is an enormous city of stars.

headline: Our Solar System Lives in the Galactic Suburbs
body: Rather than being near the center, our solar system resides about halfway out in one of the Milky Way's quieter neighborhoods.
summary: We're not located near the center of the Milky Way.

headline: One Trip Around the Milky Way Takes 250 Million Years
body: The Sun and the entire solar system need roughly 250 million years to complete one orbit around the galaxy.
summary: A single galactic year lasts 250 million Earth years.

headline: Nobody Has Ever Photographed the Milky Way From Outside
body: Because we live inside the Milky Way, humanity has never captured an actual photograph of our galaxy from the outside.
summary: We've never seen our galaxy from the outside.

headline: The Milky Way Appears as a Stripe Across the Sky
body: The glowing band visible on dark nights is actually the disk of our galaxy seen from within.
summary: The Milky Way's appearance comes from living inside it.

headline: Another Galaxy Is Heading Straight Toward Us
body: The Andromeda Galaxy is moving toward the Milky Way and will eventually merge with it.
summary: Andromeda is approaching the Milky Way.

headline: The Milky Way Will Collide With Andromeda
body: Scientists predict that the Milky Way and Andromeda galaxies will merge billions of years from now.
summary: Our galaxy has a collision in its future.

headline: Astronomers Mapped a Galaxy They Can't Leave
body: Scientists figured out the Milky Way's structure without ever seeing it from the outside by studying stars, gas, and dust.
summary: Astronomers reconstructed our galaxy from the inside.

headline: Looking Into Space Is Looking Into the Past
body: Because light takes time to travel, every object in space appears as it existed sometime in the past.
summary: Astronomers are always looking backward in time.

headline: The Sun We See Is Already Eight Minutes Old
body: Sunlight takes about 8.3 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we never see the Sun exactly as it is right now.
summary: Sunlight is already several minutes old when it reaches us.

headline: Even Nearby Stars Are Years in the Past
body: The nearest star beyond the Sun is over four light-years away, so we see it as it existed years ago.
summary: Nearby stars are seen in the past.

headline: Andromeda Appears 2.5 Million Years Younger
body: Because its light takes 2.5 million years to reach us, we see the Andromeda Galaxy as it existed long before humans evolved.
summary: Looking at Andromeda means seeing deep into the past.

headline: Some Galaxies Are Seen Near the Dawn of Time
body: Powerful telescopes can observe galaxies that formed only a few hundred million years after the Big Bang.
summary: Astronomers can study the infant universe.

headline: A Light-Year Measures Distance, Not Time
body: Despite the name, a light-year is a unit of distance rather than a measure of time.
summary: Light-years describe distance.

headline: Distance Is Time Travel for Astronomers
body: The farther away an object is, the farther back in time astronomers see when they observe it.
summary: Greater distances reveal earlier times.

headline: Hubble Has Seen More Than 13 Billion Years Into the Past
body: The Hubble Space Telescope has observed galaxies whose light has traveled for over 13 billion years.
summary: Hubble can observe the ancient universe.

headline: Hubble Has Been Exploring Space Across Four Decades
body: Launched in 1990, Hubble has remained active long enough to make discoveries across four different decades of scientific history.
summary: Hubble has operated across four decades.

headline: Astronauts Repaired a Telescope in Space
body: NASA astronauts performed multiple servicing missions to repair and upgrade Hubble while it orbited Earth.
summary: Astronauts kept Hubble alive with space repairs.

headline: Hubble Helped Discover Dark Energy
body: Observations made with Hubble contributed to the discovery that the universe's expansion is accelerating.
summary: Hubble played a role in discovering dark energy.

headline: Hubble Studies Planets Around Other Stars
body: Hubble has analyzed the atmospheres and properties of planets orbiting distant stars.
summary: Hubble investigates alien worlds.

headline: Hubble Circles Earth Instead of the Sun
body: Unlike Webb, Hubble travels around Earth while observing the universe.
summary: Hubble remains close to Earth.

headline: Hubble Lets Scientists Watch the Universe Change
body: Because Hubble has operated for decades, astronomers can observe changes occurring on human timescales.
summary: Hubble allows scientists to witness cosmic change.

headline: Hubble Captured Some of History's Most Famous Images
body: Many of the most recognizable images of space were taken by Hubble.
summary: Hubble produced iconic views of the universe.

headline: Hubble Changed Humanity's View of the Universe
body: NASA considers Hubble one of the most transformative observatories ever built.
summary: Hubble revolutionized astronomy.

headline: Hubble and Webb Work Together
body: The two space telescopes complement one another and often study the same objects using different capabilities.
summary: Hubble and Webb make a powerful team.

headline: Webb Had to Fold Like Origami
body: The James Webb Space Telescope was too large to fit inside its rocket and had to unfold itself in space.
summary: Webb deployed like a giant origami project.

headline: Engineers Compared Webb to a Transformer
body: Because of its complex unfolding sequence, engineers joked that Webb behaves like a Transformer in space.
summary: Webb's deployment inspired Transformer comparisons.

headline: Webb Lives a Million Miles From Earth
body: Webb operates near a special location called L2, roughly 1.5 million kilometers from Earth.
summary: Webb works far beyond Earth's orbit.

headline: Webb's Sunshield Is Like SPF One Million
body: NASA compares Webb's five-layer sunshield to sunscreen with an SPF of one million.
summary: Webb carries extraordinary sun protection.

headline: Webb Can See More Than 13 Billion Years Into the Past
body: Webb is capable of observing some of the earliest galaxies formed after the Big Bang.
summary: Webb studies the infant universe.

headline: Webb Studies Every Era of Cosmic History
body: From the first galaxies to modern planetary systems, Webb investigates the entire history of the universe.
summary: Webb explores all stages of cosmic evolution.

headline: Webb Continues Hubble's Legacy
body: Webb builds upon discoveries made by Hubble while expanding our view deeper into the universe.
summary: Webb extends Hubble's discoveries.

headline: Webb Launched on Christmas Day
body: The James Webb Space Telescope began its journey into space on December 25, 2021.
summary: Webb launched on Christmas Day.

headline: Webb Doesn't Orbit Earth
body: Unlike Hubble, Webb travels around the Sun near a special gravitational balance point.
summary: Webb follows a very different orbit.

headline: Webb Belongs to Scientists Around the World
body: Astronomers worldwide use Webb to investigate mysteries throughout the universe.
summary: Webb serves scientists around the globe.
